facts.fact: Load factor in hash maps is the ratio n/b (number of entries / array size). Default load factor in Java HashMap is 0.75. When load factor exceeds threshold, hash table rehashes and doubles capacity.

With exponential growth (doubling), the total time to insert n elements is O(n). This is because rehashing happens at operations 1, 2, 4, ..., 2^k, with cumulative copy cost of at most 2n element operations.
Java 8+ uses Red-Black trees instead of LinkedList for collision chains in hash maps, improving worst-case collision handling from O(n) to O(log n) lookup time.
Binary search bug in calculating mid=(left+right)/2 caused integer overflow and remained undetected for 20 years. Correct formula: mid = left + (right - left) / 2 prevents overflow.
DFS and BFS both have time complexity O(V+E) when using adjacency list representation. BFS uses a queue; DFS uses a stack or recursion.
Adjacency matrix representation has O(V²) time complexity for DFS/BFS and O(V²) space complexity because finding neighbors requires scanning entire row.
Kahn's algorithm for topological sort detects cycles: if any vertex's in-degree never becomes 0, the graph contains a cycle (not a DAG).
Topological sort has O(V+E) time complexity for both DFS and Kahn's algorithm approaches.

Tree structures have distinct characteristics: BSTs support ordered range queries at O(log n) on balanced trees; heaps provide O(log n) insertion/removal but O(n) lookup; tries provide O(k) string operations where k=length of key.
Trie prefix search time complexity is O(p+n) where p=length of prefix and n=number of words sharing that prefix. Insertion is O(k) where k=word length.
BFS is optimal for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs. Path can be reconstructed by tracking parent nodes during traversal.
BFS cycle detection in undirected graphs: visit all components and check if a visited node's parent is different from current parent. If found, cycle exists.

For append-heavy data structures (vectors/ArrayLists), amortized O(1) insertion is achieved with exponential growth factor. Java ArrayList uses 1.5x growth (fewer allocations, less wasted space). C++ std::vector uses 2x growth.
Vector growth with factor a has average insertion cost of a/(a-1) time and (a-1)n wasted cells. Golden ratio (1.618) and 1.5 have been proposed; 2x is standard for simplicity.
Bottom-up DP for Fibonacci achieves O(1) space by tracking only two prior values instead of full table. Converts O(n) space memoization to rolling window.

Recursive to iterative DP conversion: identify state dependencies, build solution from base cases upward in tabulation table, apply rolling-window optimization if state only depends on previous k values.
Netflix graph system (NetflixGraph) handles 650TB of graph data with millisecond latency. Processes service topology graphs for dependency analysis during incident investigation.
Airbnb uses Apache Airflow with DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) structure for task orchestration. Dependencies between tasks define graph structure.
Package managers (apt, yum) and build systems (Make, Maven) use topological sort to resolve dependencies and determine safe compilation order.
Separate chaining collision handling: allows load factor >1, inserts remain O(1), but longer chains degrade search to O(n). Java 8+ uses balanced trees instead of linked lists.
Open addressing collision handling: maintains cache locality, but suffers from clustering. Typical load factors capped at 50% (vs 100% for chaining). Used in Python dicts and JavaScript objects.

examples.example: Google Search and VS Code use tries for autocomplete. When user types 'pro', system instantly suggests 'programming', 'project', 'process' via prefix traversal.
Netflix incident response: engineers use NetflixGraph to analyze service dependencies and trace root cause. Handles 650TB data globally at millisecond latency.
During 2024-2025 placement season, 68% of top companies (Google, Amazon, Microsoft) asked at least one DFS/BFS difference question.
Airbnb data pipeline uses topological sort in Airflow DAGs to schedule 1000+ dependencies. Critical path analysis determines minimum project duration.
Java HashMap default load factor 0.75 triggers resize when 75% full. Initial capacity 16 doubles to 32 when 12 elements added (0.75 * 16 = 12).

analogies.analogy: Load factor in hash maps is like classroom occupancy: at 75% capacity, add more desks (rehash). Too high = hard to find seats (collisions); too low = wasted space.
BFS is like ripples spreading outward in water—explores all neighbors at distance 1 before moving to distance 2. DFS is like threading a maze—follow one path to the end, backtrack if stuck.
Tries are like directory structures in file systems: 'pro' is the directory, and 'programming', 'project', 'process' are files within. Prefix becomes a natural hierarchy.
Topological sort is like ordering tasks by dependencies: before baking a cake, buy ingredients first. Sort resolves 'who depends on whom' into a linear order.
Vector exponential growth is like restaurant seating: start with 4 tables. When full, rent space for 8 (not 5). Doubling spreads rehashing cost over many cheap insertions.

key_numbers.context: When size exceeds 0.75 * capacity, HashMap rehashes to double capacity.
Both achieve linear time visiting V vertices and E edges exactly once with adjacency list.
For recursion stack (DFS) or queue (BFS), at most O(V) nodes stored.

key_numbers.metric: Adjacency matrix time complexity for DFS
key_numbers.context: Must scan entire row for each vertex to find neighbors, even in sparse graphs.
k = length of key being inserted or searched; no hash collisions.
p = prefix length, n = number of words with that prefix.
Each iteration halves search space; applicable to non-array sorted spaces (answer spaces).
Due to call stack depth; iterative version is O(1).
Unbalanced BST degrades to O(n) in worst case (e.g., sorted input).
Min/max extraction is O(1); general search is O(n).
With doubling growth factor: n copies for inserts + n+n/2+n/4+... = 2n rehashing copies = 3n total.
Fewer allocations than 1.25x, less waste than 2x; balances time and space.
Can accept more elements than array size; longer chains degrade to O(n) search.
Clustering and probe distance grow rapidly above 50%.
Stored and queried at millisecond latency using NetflixGraph abstraction.
Percentage of top 100 companies (Google, Amazon, Microsoft, etc.) that asked DFS/BFS questions in 2024-2025 placement season.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash table load factor >1 always causes poor performance.
common_misconceptions.truth: Separate chaining allows load factor >1 with acceptable performance until chains become very long. Open addressing (used in Python/JS) caps at ~0.5 for clustering reasons.

common_misconceptions.misconception: DFS and BFS have different time complexities.
common_misconceptions.truth: Both are O(V+E) with adjacency lists, O(V^2) with adjacency matrices. The difference is traversal order (depth-first vs breadth-first), not runtime.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Binary search only works on arrays.
common_misconceptions.truth: Binary search applies to any sorted space with O(1) random access. Examples: answer spaces in optimization, rotated arrays, ranges in search.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Binary search mid calculation (left+right)/2 is safe.
common_misconceptions.truth: Causes integer overflow in fixed-size integers. Correct formula: mid = left + (right - left) / 2. Bug remained undetected for 20 years.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Tries use more memory than hash tables for string storage.
common_misconceptions.truth: Tries compress common prefixes (e.g., 'pro' shared by 'programming', 'project'). Trade-off: per-node overhead vs prefix reuse. Both have valid use cases.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Topological sort only applies to project management.
common_misconceptions.truth: Used in package managers (apt, yum), build systems (Make, Maven), blockchain transaction ordering, autonomous vehicles, and IoT scheduling.
Doubling spreads rehashing amortized cost over many insertions (O(1) amortized), making it efficient. Sub-geometric growth (e.g., +1) results in O(n) amortized cost.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Recursive DP is always better because it's intuitive.
common_misconceptions.truth: Recursive DP (memoization) uses O(log n) stack space. Bottom-up DP can be O(1) space with rolling window optimization, even for Fibonacci.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Kahn's algorithm and DFS topological sort have different time complexities.
common_misconceptions.truth: Both are O(V+E). Kahn's is preferred for cycle detection; DFS is preferred for recursive clarity.

common_misconceptions.misconception: BFS shortest path only works if all edges have weight 1.
common_misconceptions.truth: BFS shortest path assumes unweighted graphs (all edges cost 1). Weighted graphs require Dijkstra's algorithm.
